planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost after 3 months of cold stratification at 40°F; transplant to a shaded, moist, humus-rich location in spring. Division of established clumps in spring is the most reliable propagation method.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, deep, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Kamchatka bugbane is one of the most valuable late-season shade perennials, producing tall, elegant bottlebrush spikes of fragrant, white to pale pink flowers in September and October — a time when very few shade plants are in bloom. The attractive compound foliage emerges in spring and provides good texture through summer. Selections with dark purple-bronze foliage (such as 'Brunette', 'Hillside Black Beauty', and 'James Compton') are especially striking — the dark foliage glows in dappled shade and contrasts dramatically with nearby light-colored hostas and ferns. Performs best in cooler climates; in the warmth of zone 8, provide afternoon shade and consistent moisture. Formerly classified as Cimicifuga simplex.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or container plants in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; false goat's beard is the only native North American astilbe species and is more tolerant of summer heat than the Asian-origin hybrid astilbes commonly sold in nurseries.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade — this is the only native US species in the astilbe family and is a magnificent plant for moist, shaded spots in the mid-Atlantic and Appalachian region. The large, pinnately compound leaves are coarser-textured than hybrid astilbes, and the creamy white to yellowish flower plumes in late spring and early summer can reach 4–6 feet tall — among the tallest of any shade perennial. Excellent at the back of a shaded border or naturalized in moist woodland gardens alongside ferns, hostas, and trilliums. More heat tolerant than hybrid garden astilbes and better adapted to the variable rainfall of the Southeast. The dried plumes persist attractively into fall and winter. Unlike the rhizomatous spreading of some astilbes, this species forms upright, gradually expanding clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in shade; unlike the spreading T. cordifolia, Wherry's foamflower stays as a tight, non-running clump — ideal for defined shade borders where the stoloniferous species would be too aggressive.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; Wherry's foamflower is the clump-forming, non-spreading species of foamflower native to the southern Appalachians — a well-behaved alternative to the colonizing T. cordifolia for shade gardens where a defined edge is desired. The palmately lobed leaves are elegantly marked and develop attractive reddish fall color. Upright spikes of tiny white to pale pink flowers in spring are delicate and charming, and attract small native bees. This species has been extensively hybridized with T. cordifolia to create the many decorative 'Tiarella' cultivars offered in nurseries — most with ornate leaf markings in green, burgundy, and silver. The straight species has simpler foliage but is a sturdy, cold-hardy plant for naturalizing in shaded woodland gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; or cold stratify seeds 60 days and sow indoors in late winter. Transplant to very well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, lean, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; fringed blue star is a native amsonia of the southeastern Coastal Plain sandhills and pine barrens, adapted to the infertile, droughty, acid soils of longleaf pine habitats where few ornamental perennials survive. The very fine, needle-like foliage is distinctive among amsonias — quite different from the broad leaves of A. tabernaemontana — and turns a glowing golden-yellow in fall, one of the best fall color displays of any low-maintenance perennial. The star-shaped blue flowers in spring are typical of the genus. It performs better in poor, dry, acidic soil than in rich garden conditions — improved soil and irrigation can cause floppy growth. An excellent, low-maintenance native for dry, sandy, acidic sites in the Southeast.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to shade; Carolina ponysfoot establishes quickly in warm soil and spreads by creeping stems that root at each node — a naturally aggressive but low-growing groundcover that fills in open spots under trees.

directions: Seed or plant in moist, sandy to average soil in any light from full sun to deep shade in zones 7–11; Carolina ponysfoot is a native warm-season groundcover of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain and one of the most shade-tolerant groundcovers available for the South. The small, round to kidney-shaped leaves on creeping stems form a dense, soft carpet that stays under 3 inches tall and tolerates moderate foot traffic. In zone 9, it persists year-round as a semi-evergreen groundcover. It is frequently encountered as a native 'weed' in lawns in the Southeast where it is actually beneficial — a naturally occurring alternative to introduced groundcovers that requires no irrigation once established. The tiny greenish flowers are inconspicuous. Often sown as a grass substitute under large trees where grass will not grow.
